controls.name: CIS-5.2.2 Prefer using dedicated GCP Service Accounts and Workload Identity
controls.description: Use Workload Identity to bind Kubernetes service accounts to dedicated GCP service accounts with the minimum required IAM permissions.
controls.long_description: Workload Identity is the recommended way to access Google Cloud APIs from GKE. It eliminates the need for service account key files and provides per-workload identity, enabling fine-grained IAM permissions for individual workloads.
controls.remediation:
**Enable Workload Identity on the cluster:**

```
gcloud container clusters update CLUSTER_NAME \
  --workload-pool=PROJECT_ID.svc.id.goog \
  --region REGION
```

**Enable on node pools:**

```
gcloud container node-pools update NODE_POOL \
  --cluster=CLUSTER_NAME \
  --workload-metadata=GKE_METADATA \
  --region REGION
```

**Bind a Kubernetes SA to a GCP SA:**

```
gcloud iam service-accounts add-iam-policy-binding GCP_SA_EMAIL \
  --role=roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser \
  --member=serviceAccount:PROJECT_ID.svc.id.goog[NAMESPACE/KSA_NAME]
```
controls.manual_test:
For each namespace in the cluster, review the rights assigned to the default service account and ensure that it has no roles or cluster roles bound to it apart from the defaults.

 Additionally ensure that the automountServiceAccountToken: false setting is in place for each default service account.

rules.name: ensure-default-service-accounts-has-only-default-roles
automount-default-service-account
rules.description: fails if default service account mounts service account token by default
rules.remediation: Make sure that the automountServiceAccountToken field on the default service account spec is set to false

controls.name: CIS-5.3.1 Ensure Kubernetes Secrets are encrypted using keys managed in Cloud KMS
controls.description: Enable application-layer secrets encryption using a Cloud KMS customer-managed key (CMEK) for GKE Secrets stored in etcd.
controls.remediation:
**Create a Cloud KMS key:**

```
gcloud kms keyrings create KEYRING_NAME --location=LOCATION
gcloud kms keys create KEY_NAME \
  --location=LOCATION \
  --keyring=KEYRING_NAME \
  --purpose=encryption
```

**Enable on an existing cluster:**

```
gcloud container clusters update CLUSTER_NAME \
  --database-encryption-key=projects/PROJECT_ID/locations/LOCATION/keyRings/KEYRING_NAME/cryptoKeys/KEY_NAME \
  --region REGION
```
controls.long_description: By default, GKE encrypts Secrets at rest using Google-managed keys. Enabling application-layer encryption with a customer-managed Cloud KMS key provides an additional layer of control and auditability over Secret encryption.
controls.test: Reading the cluster description from the managed cloud API (EKS, GKE), or the API server pod configuration for native K8s and checking if etcd encryption is enabled

rules.name: secret-etcd-encryption-cloud
etcd-encryption-native
rules.rule:
package armo_builtins

import data.cautils

# Check if encryption in etcd is enabled for native k8s
deny[msga] {
	apiserverpod := input[_]
	cmd := apiserverpod.spec.containers[0].command
	enc_command := [command | command := cmd[_]; contains(command, "--encryption-provider-config=")]
	count(enc_command) < 1
	fixpath := {"path":sprintf("spec.containers[0].command[%d]", [count(cmd)]), "value": "--encryption-provider-config=YOUR_VALUE"}

	msga := {
		"alertMessage": "etcd encryption is not enabled",
		"alertScore": 9,
		"packagename": "armo_builtins",
		"failedPaths": [],
		"fixPaths": [fixpath],
		"alertObject": {"k8sApiObjects": [apiserverpod]},
	}
}

controls.name: CIS-5.5.1 Ensure Container-Optimized OS (cos_containerd) is used for GKE Node images
controls.description: Use Container-Optimized OS (cos_containerd) as the node image for GKE node pools.
controls.long_description: Container-Optimized OS is a hardened operating system image optimised for running containers. It has a minimal footprint, read-only root filesystem, and includes built-in security features. cos_containerd is the recommended variant using containerd as the container runtime.
controls.remediation:
**From Google Cloud Console:**

1. Go to Kubernetes Engine > Clusters.
2. Click the cluster, then select the node pool.
3. Set 'Image type' to 'Container-Optimized OS with containerd (cos_containerd)'.

**From gcloud CLI:**

```
gcloud container node-pools create NODE_POOL \
  --image-type=COS_CONTAINERD \
  --cluster=CLUSTER_NAME \
  --region REGION
```
controls.manual_test: If a container-optimized OS is required examine the nodes in EC2 and click on their AMI to ensure that it is a container-optimized OS like Amazon Bottlerocket; or connect to the worker node and check its OS.
controls.impact_statement: A container-optimized OS may have limited or no support for package managers, execution of non-containerized applications, or ability to install third-party drivers or kernel modules. Conventional remote access to the host (i.e. ssh) may not be possible, with access and debugging being intended via a management tool.
controls.default_value: A container-optimized OS is not the default.

rules.name: alert-container-optimized-os-not-in-use
rules.rule:
package armo_builtins
import future.keywords.in


# checks if a node is not using a "Container-Optimized OS". 
# "Container-Optimized OS" prefixes are configured in 'container_optimized_os_prefixes'.  
# deny if 'nodes.status.nodeInfo.osImage' not starting with at least one item in 'container_optimized_os_prefixes'.
deny[msga] {

	nodes := input[_]
	nodes.kind == "Node"

	# list of "Container-Optimized OS" images prefixes 
	container_optimized_os_prefixes = ["Bottlerocket"]

	# check if osImage starts with at least one prefix
	some str in container_optimized_os_prefixes
	not startswith(nodes.status.nodeInfo.osImage, str)

	# prepare message data.
	alert_message :=  "Prefer using Container-Optimized OS when possible"

	failedPaths:= ["status.nodeInfo.osImage"]

	msga := {
		"alertMessage": alert_message,
		"packagename": "armo_builtins",

		"alertScore": 7,
		"reviewPaths": failedPaths,
		"failedPaths": failedPaths,
		"fixPaths": [],
		"alertObject": {
			"k8sApiObjects": [nodes]
		}
	}
}

controls.name: CIS-5.6.3 Ensure Control Plane Authorized Networks is Enabled
controls.description: Enable authorized networks to restrict access to the GKE control plane endpoint to specific CIDR ranges.
controls.long_description: By default the GKE control plane is accessible from any IP address. Enabling authorized networks restricts access to the Kubernetes API server to trusted IP ranges, reducing the attack surface.
controls.remediation:
**From Google Cloud Console:**

1. Go to Kubernetes Engine > Clusters.
2. Click the cluster name, then click 'Edit'.
3. Under 'Networking', enable 'Control plane authorized networks'.
4. Add the trusted CIDR ranges.

**From gcloud CLI:**

```
gcloud container clusters update CLUSTER_NAME \
  --enable-master-authorized-networks \
  --master-authorized-networks CIDR_RANGE \
  --region REGION
```
controls.impact_statement: When implementing Endpoint Private Access, be careful to ensure all desired networks are on the allowlist (whitelist) to prevent inadvertently blocking external access to your cluster's control plane.
controls.default_value: By default, Endpoint Public Access is disabled.

rules.name: ensure-endpointprivateaccess-is-enabled
rules.rule:
package armo_builtins


# Check if EndpointPrivateAccess in disabled for EKS
deny[msga] {
	cluster_config := input[_]
	cluster_config.apiVersion == "eks.amazonaws.com/v1"
	cluster_config.kind == "ClusterDescribe"
    cluster_config.metadata.provider == "eks"	
	config = cluster_config.data

	config.Cluster.ResourcesVpcConfig.EndpointPrivateAccess == false    
	
	msga := {
		"alertMessage": "endpointPrivateAccess is not enabled",
		"alertScore": 3,
		"packagename": "armo_builtins",
		"failedPaths": [],
		"fixPaths": [],
		"fixCommand": "aws eks update-cluster-config --region $AWS_REGION --name $CLUSTER_NAME --resources-vpc-config endpointPrivateAccess=true,endpointPublicAccess=false",
		"alertObject": {
			"k8sApiObjects": [],
            "externalObjects": cluster_config
		}
	}
}

controls.name: CIS-5.6.4 Ensure clusters are created with Private Endpoint Enabled and Public Access Disabled
controls.description: Create GKE clusters with the control plane accessible only via a private endpoint and public access disabled.
controls.long_description: Disabling public access to the GKE control plane ensures that the Kubernetes API server is only reachable from within the VPC or via authorised private connections, significantly reducing the attack surface.
controls.remediation:
**From gcloud CLI (cluster creation):**

```
gcloud container clusters create CLUSTER_NAME \
  --enable-private-endpoint \
  --enable-private-nodes \
  --master-ipv4-cidr 172.16.0.32/28 \
  --region REGION
```

Note: This setting can only be configured at cluster creation time.
controls.manual_test: Check for private endpoint access to the Kubernetes API server
controls.impact_statement:
Configure the EKS cluster endpoint to be private.

 1. Leave the cluster endpoint public and specify which CIDR blocks can communicate with the cluster endpoint. The blocks are effectively a whitelisted set of public IP addresses that are allowed to access the cluster endpoint.
2. Configure public access with a set of whitelisted CIDR blocks and set private endpoint access to enabled. This will allow public access from a specific range of public IPs while forcing all network traffic between the kubelets (workers) and the Kubernetes API through the cross-account ENIs that get provisioned into the cluster VPC when the control plane is provisioned.
controls.default_value: By default, the Public Endpoint is disabled.

rules.name: ensure-endpointprivateaccess-is-enabled-and-endpointpublicaccess-is-disabled-eks

controls.name: CIS-5.6.5 Ensure clusters are created with Private Nodes
controls.description: Create GKE node pools with private nodes so that worker nodes do not have public IP addresses.
controls.long_description: Worker nodes with public IP addresses are directly reachable from the internet. Using private nodes ensures that worker nodes only have internal IP addresses, limiting exposure and reducing the blast radius of a compromised node.
controls.remediation:
**From gcloud CLI (cluster creation):**

```
gcloud container clusters create CLUSTER_NAME \
  --enable-private-nodes \
  --master-ipv4-cidr 172.16.0.32/28 \
  --region REGION
```

Note: Private nodes must be enabled at cluster creation.
controls.impact_statement:
To enable Private Nodes, the cluster has to also be configured with a private master IP range and IP Aliasing enabled.

 Private Nodes do not have outbound access to the public internet. If you want to provide outbound Internet access for your private nodes, you can use Cloud NAT or you can manage your own NAT gateway.

rules.name: ensure-endpointpublicaccess-is-disabled-on-private-nodes-eks

package armo_builtins

# Check if EndpointPublicAccess in enabled on a private node for EKS. A private node is a node with no public ips access.

		"alertObject": {
			"k8sApiObjects": [],
            "externalObjects": cluster_config
		}
	}
}

controls.name: CIS-5.6.7 Ensure use of Google-managed SSL Certificates
controls.description: Use Google-managed SSL certificates for HTTPS load balancers fronting GKE workloads.
controls.long_description: Google-managed SSL certificates are automatically provisioned, renewed, and managed by Google. Using them ensures HTTPS load balancers always have valid, up-to-date certificates without manual certificate lifecycle management.
controls.remediation:
Create a ManagedCertificate resource and attach it to your Ingress:

```yaml
apiVersion: networking.gke.io/v1
kind: ManagedCertificate
metadata:
  name: managed-cert
spec:
  domains:
    - example.com
```

Annotate your Ingress:

```yaml
annotations:
  networking.gke.io/managed-certificates: managed-cert
```

rules.name: ensure-https-loadbalancers-encrypted-with-tls-aws
rules.rule:
package armo_builtins

import data.kubernetes.api.client

# deny LoadBalancer services that are configured for ssl connection (port: 443), but don't have TLS certificate set.
deny[msga] {

	wl_kind := "Service"
	wl_type := "LoadBalancer"
	wl_required_annotation := "service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-ssl-cert"

	# filterring LoadBalancers
	wl := 	input[_]
	wl.kind == wl_kind
	wl.spec.type == wl_type

	#  filterring loadbalancers with port 443.
	wl.spec.ports[_].port == 443

	# filterring annotations without ssl cert confgiured.
	annotations := object.get(wl, ["metadata", "annotations"], [])
	ssl_cert_annotations := [annotations[i] | annotation = i; startswith(i, wl_required_annotation)]
	count(ssl_cert_annotations) == 0

	# prepare message data.
	alert_message :=  sprintf("LoadBalancer '%v' has no TLS configured", [wl.metadata.name])
	failed_paths := []
	fixed_paths := [{"path": sprintf("metadata.annotations['%v']", [wl_required_annotation]), "value": "AWS_LOADBALANCER_SSL_CERT"}]

	msga := {
		"alertMessage": alert_message,
		"packagename": "armo_builtins",
		"alertScore": 7,
		"failedPaths": failed_paths,
		"fixPaths": fixed_paths,
		"alertObject": {
			"k8sApiObjects": [],
            "externalObjects": wl
		}
	}
}

controls.name: CIS-5.8.1 Ensure authentication using Client Certificates is Disabled
controls.description: Disable client certificate authentication to the GKE API server and use stronger authentication mechanisms.
controls.long_description: Client certificate authentication is a legacy mechanism that is harder to manage and rotate than modern alternatives such as OIDC or Workload Identity. Disabling it reduces the risk of compromised long-lived credentials.
controls.remediation:
Client certificate authentication cannot be disabled on existing clusters. Create new clusters with the flag:

```
gcloud container clusters create CLUSTER_NAME \
  --no-enable-basic-auth \
  --region REGION
```

Verify no client certificates are issued:

```
gcloud container clusters describe CLUSTER_NAME \
  --region REGION \
  --format='value(masterAuth.clientCertificate)'
```

The output should be empty.
controls.manual_test: Review user access to the cluster and ensure that users are not making use of Kubernetes client certificate authentication.
controls.impact_statement: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
controls.default_value: Client certificate authentication is enabled by default.

rules.name: rule-manual
rules.description: Due to the difficulty of performing a good check, the review is left manual to the user.

controls.name: CIS-5.8.2 Manage Kubernetes RBAC users for GKE with Google groups in Google Workspace
controls.description: Use Google Groups for GKE (G Suite groups) to manage RBAC access, binding ClusterRoles to Google Workspace groups instead of individual user accounts.
controls.long_description: Binding Kubernetes RBAC roles to Google Workspace groups rather than individual user accounts simplifies access management. When a user joins or leaves a group, their cluster access is automatically updated without needing to modify RBAC bindings.
controls.remediation:
**Enable Google Groups for RBAC at cluster creation:**

```
gcloud container clusters create CLUSTER_NAME \
  --security-group=gke-security-groups@DOMAIN.com \
  --region REGION
```

**Bind a ClusterRole to a Google Group:**

```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: group-binding
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view
subjects:
- apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Group
  name: GROUP_EMAIL@DOMAIN.com
```
controls.manual_test:
To Audit access to the namespace $NAMESPACE, assume the IAM role yourIAMRoleName for a user that you created, and then run the following command:

 
```
$ kubectl get role -n $NAMESPACE

```
 The response lists the RBAC role that has access to this Namespace.
controls.impact_statement: Users must now be assigned to the IAM group created to use this namespace and deploy applications. If they are not they will not be able to access the namespace or deploy.
controls.default_value: For role-based access control (RBAC), system:masters permissions are configured in the Amazon EKS control plane

controls.name: CIS-5.8.3 Ensure Legacy Authorization (ABAC) is Disabled
controls.description: Ensure that legacy Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) is disabled and RBAC is used for cluster authorization.
controls.remediation:
**From gcloud CLI:**

Verify ABAC is disabled:

```
gcloud container clusters describe CLUSTER_NAME \
  --region REGION \
  --format='value(legacyAbac.enabled)'
```

The output should be empty or 'False'. To disable if enabled:

```
gcloud container clusters update CLUSTER_NAME \
  --no-enable-legacy-authorization \
  --region REGION
```
controls.long_description: ABAC is a legacy authorization mechanism that is less flexible and auditable than RBAC. On GKE, ABAC is disabled by default on clusters running Kubernetes 1.8 and later. Verify it is not re-enabled.
controls.test: Testing API server or managed Kubernetes vendor API to determine if RBAC is enabled

rules.name: rbac-enabled-cloud
rules.rule:
package armo_builtins

deny[msga] {
	cluster_config := input[_]
	cluster_config.apiVersion == "management.azure.com/v1"
	cluster_config.kind == "ClusterDescribe"
	cluster_config.metadata.provider == "aks"
	config := cluster_config.data
	not config.properties.enableRBAC == true

	msga := {
		"alertMessage": "rbac is not enabled",
		"alertScore": 3,
		"packagename": "armo_builtins",
		"reviewPaths": ["data.properties.enableRBAC"],
		"failedPaths": ["data.properties.enableRBAC"],
		"fixCommand": "",
		"fixPaths": [],
		"alertObject": {
            		"externalObjects": cluster_config
		}
	}
}

rules.name: rbac-enabled-native
rules.rule:
package armo_builtins


# Check if psp is enabled for native k8s
deny[msga] {
	apiserverpod := input[_]
    cmd := apiserverpod.spec.containers[0].command[j]
    contains(cmd, "--authorization-mode=")
    output := split(cmd, "=")
    not contains(output[1], "RBAC")
	path := sprintf("spec.containers[0].command[%v]", [format_int(j, 10)])	
	
	msga := {
		"alertMessage": "RBAC is not enabled",
		"alertScore": 9,
		"packagename": "armo_builtins",
		"reviewPaths": [path],
		"failedPaths": [path],
		"fixPaths": [],
		"alertObject": {
			"k8sApiObjects": [apiserverpod],
		}
	}
}

controls.name: CIS-5.10.1 Ensure Kubernetes Web UI is Disabled
controls.description: Disable the Kubernetes Dashboard on GKE clusters.
controls.remediation:
**From gcloud CLI:**

Verify the dashboard is disabled:

```
gcloud container clusters describe CLUSTER_NAME \
  --region REGION \
  --format='value(addonsConfig.kubernetesDashboard.disabled)'
```

The output should be 'True'. If the dashboard addon is enabled:

```
gcloud container clusters update CLUSTER_NAME \
  --update-addons=KubernetesDashboard=DISABLED \
  --region REGION
```
controls.long_description: The Kubernetes Dashboard provides a web-based UI for managing cluster resources. It can be a security risk if exposed or improperly secured. GKE disables it by default on newer cluster versions; ensure it is not re-enabled.
controls.test: Check who is associated with the dashboard service account or bound to dashboard role/clusterrole.

rules.name: rule-access-dashboard-subject-v1

attributes.m$K8sThreatMatrix: Lateral Movement::Access Kubernetes dashboard, Discovery::Access Kubernetes dashboard

rules.description: fails if subject that is not dashboard service account is bound to dashboard role/clusterrole, or- if anyone that is not dashboard pod is associated with its service account.
rules.rule:
package armo_builtins

# input: regoResponseVectorObject
# fails if a subject that is not dashboard service account is bound to dashboard role

deny[msga] {
	subjectVector := input[_]
	role := subjectVector.relatedObjects[i]
	rolebinding := subjectVector.relatedObjects[j]
	endswith(subjectVector.relatedObjects[i].kind, "Role")
	endswith(subjectVector.relatedObjects[j].kind, "Binding")

	role.metadata.name == "kubernetes-dashboard"
	subjectVector.name != "kubernetes-dashboard"

	subject := rolebinding.subjects[k]
    path := [sprintf("relatedObjects[%v].subjects[%v]", [format_int(j, 10), format_int(k, 10)])]
	finalpath := array.concat(path, [sprintf("relatedObjects[%v].roleRef.name", [format_int(j, 10)])])
	msga := {
		"alertMessage": sprintf("Subject: %v-%v is bound to dashboard role/clusterrole", [subjectVector.kind, subjectVector.name]),
		"alertScore": 9,
		"reviewPaths": finalpath,
		"failedPaths": finalpath,
		"fixPaths": [],
		"packagename": "armo_builtins",
		"alertObject": {
			"k8sApiObjects": [],
			"externalObjects": subjectVector
		}
	}
}

controls.name: CIS-5.10.3 Consider GKE Sandbox for running untrusted workloads
controls.description: Use GKE Sandbox (powered by gVisor) to provide stronger isolation for untrusted or multi-tenant workloads.
controls.long_description: GKE Sandbox provides an additional layer of security between containerised workloads and the host kernel using gVisor. It is recommended for running untrusted code, multi-tenant workloads, or workloads processing sensitive data.
controls.remediation:
**Enable GKE Sandbox on a node pool:**

```
gcloud container node-pools create SANDBOX_POOL \
  --cluster=CLUSTER_NAME \
  --sandbox type=gvisor \
  --region REGION
```

**Schedule workloads on sandbox nodes using a RuntimeClass:**

```yaml
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: gvisor
handler: runsc
```
controls.default_value: By default, AWS Fargate is not utilized.

rules.name: alert-fargate-not-in-use
rules.rule:
package armo_builtins




# deny if fargate is not being used in any of the nodes in cluster.
# a Node is identified as using fargate if it's name starts with 'fargate'.
deny[msga] {


    # get all nodes
    nodes := [node | node = input[_]; node.kind == "Node"]
    count(nodes) > 0

    # get all nodes without fargate
    nodes_not_fargate := [node | node = nodes[_]; not startswith(node.metadata.name, "fargate")]

    # if count of all nodes equals to count of nodes_not_fargate it means fargate is not being used.
    count(nodes) == count(nodes_not_fargate)

	# prepare message data.
	alert_message :=  "Consider Fargate for running untrusted workloads"

	msga := {
		"alertMessage": alert_message,
		"packagename": "armo_builtins",

		"alertScore": 7,
		"failedPaths": [],
		"fixPaths": [],
		"alertObject": {
			"k8sApiObjects": nodes_not_fargate
		}
	}
}
